chunk_summary: Guidelines for triaging children with SAM into inpatient or outpatient care based on complications, danger signs, and caregiver capacity, with special considerations for oedema and practical barriers.
learning_objectives: Differentiate between complicated and uncomplicated SAM based on clinical criteria.
Understand the role of danger signs and appetite tests in SAM triage.
Recognize the implications of oedema severity in SAM management pathways.
Identify caregiver-related barriers that may necessitate inpatient care.
key_facts: Complicated SAM includes medical complications or failed appetite tests and requires inpatient care.
Danger signs include fever >39°C, hypoglycemia, hypothermia, respiratory distress, and severe dehydration or shock.
Severe oedema (+++) is an automatic inpatient indication in Indian protocols.
Uncomplicated SAM requires clinical stability, no danger signs, and a passed appetite test.
Caregiver barriers, such as inability to administer feeds or unsafe hygiene, justify inpatient care.
reference_quote: Severe oedema (+++) is an automatic inpatient indication in Indian protocols. The 2023 WHO guideline allows trained community teams to treat selected children with nutritional oedema outside hospital, but outpatient system reliability varies across India.
document_key_topics: Danger signs and appetite tests in SAM triage
